texts.orig: Learning to Refine Input Constrained Control Barrier Functions via Uncertainty-Aware Online Parameter Adaptation
texts.text: Learning to Refine Input Constrained Control Barrier Functions via Uncertainty-Aware Online Parameter Adaptation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Taekyung Kim, Robin Inho Kee and Dimitra Panagou
texts.text: Taekyung Kim, Robin Inho Kee and Dimitra Panagou

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract -Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) have become powerful tools for ensuring safety in nonlinear systems. However, finding valid CBFs that guarantee persistent safety and feasibility remains an open challenge, especially in systems with input constraints. Traditional approaches often rely on manually tuning the parameters of the class K functions of the CBF conditions a priori. The performance of CBFbased controllers is highly sensitive to these fixed parameters, potentially leading to overly conservative behavior or safety violations. To overcome these issues, this paper introduces a learning-based optimal control framework for online adaptation of Input Constrained CBF (ICCBF) parameters in discretetime nonlinear systems. Our method employs a probabilistic ensemble neural network to predict the performance and risk metrics, as defined in this work, for candidate parameters, accounting for both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties. We propose a two-step verification process using Jensen-R´ enyi Divergence and distributionally-robust Conditional Value at Risk to identify valid parameters. This enables dynamic refinement of ICCBF parameters based on current state and nearby environments, optimizing performance while ensuring safety within the verified parameter set. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms both fixed-parameter and existing adaptive methods in robot navigation scenarios across safety and performance metrics. Our method employs a probabilistic ensemble neural network to predict the performance and risk metrics, as defined in this work, for candidate parameters, accounting for both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties. We propose a two-step verification process using Jensen-R´ enyi Divergence and distributionally-robust Conditional Value at Risk to identify valid parameters. This enables dynamic refinement of ICCBF parameters based on current state and nearby environments, optimizing performance while ensuring safety within the verified parameter set. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Safety-critical optimal control is one of the fundamental challenges in robotics, e.g., robots need to navigate safely around obstacles while optimizing various criteria such as energy consumption. Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) have been developed as a tool to encode safety for nonlinear systems and applied to high-level planning and multi-agent systems [1]-[9]. High Order CBFs extend this concept to systems with constraints of higher relative degree with respect to the system dynamics [10]-[13]. However, a significant challenge lies in finding valid CBFs under input constraints [14]. Functions that do not provide persistent safety and feasibility guarantees are often referred to as candidate CBFs [15].
texts.text: Safety-critical optimal control is one of the fundamental challenges in robotics, e.g., robots need to navigate safely around obstacles while optimizing various criteria such as energy consumption. Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) have been developed as a tool to encode safety for nonlinear systems and applied to high-level planning and multi-agent systems [1]-[9]. High Order CBFs extend this concept to systems with constraints of higher relative degree with respect to the system dynamics [10]-[13]. However, a significant challenge lies in finding valid CBFs under input constraints [14]. Functions that do not provide persistent safety and feasibility guarantees are often referred to as candidate CBFs [15].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent efforts in the third category have focused on adapting CBFs' class K function parameters online [31]. Some approaches [32], [33] optimize decay rates of CBF constraints to guarantee point-wise feasibility, but fall short of guaranteeing safety. More recently, learning-based methods have become increasingly popular. However, their safety guarantees rely on unrealistic assumptions such as unbounded control inputs [34] or perfect learned models [35], [36].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To address these limitations, we propose a learning-based framework for online adaptation of CBF class K function parameters in input-constrained systems. We leverage a probabilistic ensemble neural network (PENN) to predict the safety and performance characteristics of potential parameter configurations while considering both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties. The parameters are dynamically adapted based on the current system state and nearby environmental configurations, simultaneously ensuring safety and optimizing performance for navigation tasks. We quantify epistemic uncertainty, which originates from insufficient training data, using Jensen-R´ enyi Divergence, discarding predictions with low confidence. To account for aleatoric uncertainty, arising from sources such as data ambiguity and measurement noise, we model predicted outputs as distributions. We then employ distributionally robust Conditional Value at Risk to assess the risk associated with these predictions.
texts.text: To address these limitations, we propose a learning-based framework for online adaptation of CBF class K function parameters in input-constrained systems. We leverage a probabilistic ensemble neural network (PENN) to predict the safety and performance characteristics of potential parameter configurations while considering both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties. The parameters are dynamically adapted based on the current system state and nearby environmental configurations, simultaneously ensuring safety and optimizing performance for navigation tasks. We quantify epistemic uncertainty, which originates from insufficient training data, using Jensen-R´ enyi Divergence, discarding predictions with low confidence. To account for aleatoric uncertainty, arising from sources such as data ambiguity and measurement noise, we model predicted outputs as distributions. We then employ distributionally robust Conditional Value at Risk to assess the risk associated with these predictions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the original ICCBF approach, which is common in many CBF applications, the objective is to identify a fixed set of class K functions α that make the function b r a valid ICCBF for all states in the inner safe set C ∗ . However, this approach presents two main challenges: a) it is difficult to find such a set, and b) it can be overly conservative, potentially restricting the system's performance.

However, this approach presents two main challenges: a) it is difficult to find such a set, and b) it can be overly conservative, potentially restricting the system's performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To address these limitations, we propose an online adaptive approach that seeks to find a set of class K functions that make the ICCBF candidate function b r to be locally valid (to be formally defined in this section) at each control iteration, based on the current state and the surrounding environment. This method eliminates the need to exhaustively search over all possible class K functions α and validate (8) with the corresponding set C ∗ ( α ) . Moreover, it allows the ICCBF to be dynamically tailored to the specific conditions the system encounters in real time.

It is obvious that a controller with a hard constraint like (16) cannot ensure safety from all initial conditions. The ICCBF constraint value in (9d) is a useful metric to measure the safety margin with respect to the current robot state x t and the system dynamics (1). However, the rate of change of the original hard constraint h in a discrete-time setting is computed via the time difference of subsequent value, it does not directly encode the direction of the robot's motion. Therefore, inspired by [43], we introduce a safety loss density function that incorporates both spatial state and directional information to provide a more comprehensive measure of collision risk.

At each subsequent time step, the algorithm generates K candidate class K function parameters A cand = { α ( k ) } K k =1 by sampling uniformly around the previous optimal values. To identify locally valid class K functions A valid , the algorithm filters these predictions based on two criteria as described in Sec. IV-D.

By repeating this process at each time step, it continuously refines the ICCBF parameters based on the current state and the predictions from the PENN model, thereby optimizing performance while maintaining safety.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider a system has constrained inputs (9c) as a ∈ [ -0 . 5 , 0 . 5] m/s 2 and ω ∈ [ -0 . 5 , 0 . 5] rad/s. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. Online Adaptive MPC-ICCBF
texts.text: B. Online Adaptive MPC-ICCBF

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We compare the performance of our method with four different approaches: MPC-ICCBF using 1) 'Low' ( γ 0 = γ 1 = 0 . 01 ) and 2) 'High' ( γ 0 = γ 1 = 0 . 2 ) fixed class K function parameters, 3) Optimal-decay CBF-QP [32] that can optimize class K functions online (abbreviated as 'Opt. QP'), and 4) 'Optimal-decay MPC-CBF' [33] where it incorporates the same optimization technique as [32] within the MPC structure (abbreviated as 'Opt. MPC'). For 3) and 4), we optimize both γ 0 and γ 1 and penalize them in the cost function. For our method, we empirically set the adaptation range as γ 0 , γ 1 ∈ (0 . 01 , 0 . 2) . As demonstrated in [14], when the relative degree of the constraint function h with respect to the control input matches the order to which the ICCBF is constructed, the ICCBF trivially reduces to an HOCBF in continuous time. Therefore, we adopt a discretetime HOCBF implementation in our experiments.
texts.text: We compare the performance of our method with four different approaches: MPC-ICCBF using 1) 'Low' ( γ 0 = γ 1 = 0 . 01 ) and 2) 'High' ( γ 0 = γ 1 = 0 . 2 ) fixed class K function parameters, 3) Optimal-decay CBF-QP [32] that can optimize class K functions online (abbreviated as 'Opt. QP'), and 4) 'Optimal-decay MPC-CBF' [33] where it incorporates the same optimization technique as [32] within the MPC structure (abbreviated as 'Opt. MPC'). For 3) and 4), we optimize both γ 0 and γ 1 and penalize them in the cost function. For our method, we empirically set the adaptation range as γ 0 , γ 1 ∈ (0 . 01 , 0 . 2) . As demonstrated in [14], when the relative degree of the constraint function h with respect to the control input matches the order to which the ICCBF is constructed, the ICCBF trivially reduces to an HOCBF in continuous time. Therefore, we adopt a discretetime HOCBF implementation in our experiments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The conservative approach, using 'Low' ICCBF parameters, is significantly affected by the initial nearby obstacle due to the ICCBF constraints. In some cases, the high initial velocities v 0 ∈ x 0 result in states that fall outside the inner safe set defined by fixed class K functions, leading to collisions. The aggressive approach with 'High' ICCBF parameters becomes infeasible in early iterations and eventually collides with obstacles. While 'Opt. QP' shows fewer collisions, it is prone to deadlock. 'Opt. MPC' avoids infeasibility but still results in collisions, as it only guarantees the pointwise feasibility of the solution rather than persistent safety. In contrast, our proposed method actively refines the class K function parameters online based on the PENN model's predictions. It successfully reaches the goal without collisions or deadlocks, achieving the highest goal-reaching rate in both scenarios and demonstrating its ability to optimize performance while ensuring safety.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: VI. CONCLUSION
texts.text: VI. CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we present an online class K function parameters adaptation approach in discrete-time ICCBFs. To this end, we first introduce the concept of locally valid class K functions, which ensure forward invariance for a smaller subset of the inner safe set. By leveraging a PENN model with a novel two-step verification process that accounts for both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties, our method can dynamically refine the ICCBF parameters to ensure safety and optimize MPC performance. Experimental results demonstrate that it outperforms the compared baselines in collision avoidance and goal-reaching rates across various scenarios. Future work may focus on enhancing multi-obstacle handling through graph neural network feature encoding, which could improve scalability to varying numbers of obstacles. Also, extending our method to higherdimensional systems, such as quadrotors, is another area of interest.
texts.text: In this paper, we present an online class K function parameters adaptation approach in discrete-time ICCBFs. To this end, we first introduce the concept of locally valid class K functions, which ensure forward invariance for a smaller subset of the inner safe set. By leveraging a PENN model with a novel two-step verification process that accounts for both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties, our method can dynamically refine the ICCBF parameters to ensure safety and optimize MPC performance. Experimental results demonstrate that it outperforms the compared baselines in collision avoidance and goal-reaching rates across various scenarios. Future work may focus on enhancing multi-obstacle handling through graph neural network feature encoding, which could improve scalability to varying numbers of obstacles. Also, extending our method to higherdimensional systems, such as quadrotors, is another area of interest.
